document.critique: Rechecked all machine counts, eligibility, pairwise constraints, implications, and exact-one rules. Candidate 2 survives every test: each machine has three jobs and its cost sums to 166. Candidates 0, 3, and 4 are feasible but cost 167, 182, and 176 respectively; Candidate 1 violates counts and J1/J3 separation. Thus retain the preceding proposal.

Rechecked all machine counts, domain limits, pairwise constraints, conditionals, and both exact-one rules. Candidates 0 and 2 are feasible at 117 and 104; candidate 1 fails because J1=X requires J5≠Y. Candidate 3 is feasible, satisfies three jobs per machine, and costs 103, so the preceding proposal should be retained as the unique lowest-cost answer.

Tested machine counts, all pairwise and conditional rules, both exact-one constraints, and recomputed costs. Candidate 3 is feasible at 165. Candidate 0 fails because J4=Z requires J9≠Z; candidate 2 has counts 2/4 on X/Z. Candidates 1 and 4 survive but cost 203 and 168. Retain the preceding proposal as uniquely cheapest.

I tested all five candidates against machine counts, pairwise constraints, conditional rules, and both exact-one conditions. Candidates 2 and 3 fail because J7=Y while J4=X. Candidates 0, 1, and 4 survive, with exact costs 148, 146, and 149. Thus candidate 1 is uniquely best and the preceding proposal should be retained.

Rechecked capacities, all restrictions, conditionals, and both exact-one rules. Candidate 0 fails because J9=Z requires J12≠W; candidate 1 has zero of J9,J2,J12 on Z; candidates 2 and 3 survive, costing 144 and 150. Candidate 2 is therefore optimal among the provided assignments and should retain the preceding proposal.

Tested all capacities, costs, pairwise interactions, implications, and both exact-one rules. Candidate 0 survives: each machine has three jobs and its cost is 178. Candidates 1–3 fail because J9=Z requires J10≠W, yet each assigns J10=W. The returned answer retains the preceding proposal.

I re-evaluated every speaker formula under the preceding assignment and all five candidates. The preceding proposal fails B (its XOR statement is false despite B being marked truthful), while each candidate also contains at least one type/formula mismatch. Candidate 0 is the surviving exact assignment after checking all 17 equations; in particular its nested implications and parity/count conditions agree with every listed type. It therefore replaces the preceding proposal.

I rechecked all 17 nested statements. The preceding proposal fails at N: N's statement evaluates true despite N being labeled liar. Changing N to truthful makes H's statement false, so H must change to liar; the remaining 15 evaluations then match their labels. All candidates 0–4 independently fail, so this corrected answer replaces the proposal.

I re-evaluated all five candidates against every nested statement. Candidates 0–3 each fail at least one equation. Candidate 4 also fails: E’s statement evaluates false although E is marked truthful, and G’s evaluates true although G is marked liar. Thus no candidate is an exact solution; this mapping is retained only as the preceding proposal/closest candidate, not as a validated exact answer.

I tested all five candidates by evaluating each speaker’s nested statement against the proposed type. Every candidate fails: in particular, Candidate 3 (the preceding proposal) makes E’s statement true while labeling E a liar. Therefore the candidates and preceding proposal do not establish a valid exact answer; this returned mapping is not justified as a unique solution.

I re-evaluated every nested statement under the proposed types. The preceding proposal fails at A, and its implied assignment also fails multiple additional equations. Testing the coupled Boolean fixed point yields the assignment above: each statement evaluates true exactly for truthful people and false exactly for liars, so this replaces the preceding proposal.

I recomputed all statements, including the decisive G, H, N, and P equations. Candidate 3 does not survive: changing H makes C false, and further dependencies fail. Candidate 0 fails at J; candidate 2 fails at E. Candidate 1 satisfies the remaining checked equations and is the best corrected replacement, though N requires careful validation because its statement is true under the listed values.

I re-derived the residue-class difference equations and tested all allowed p[0], q[0], t, and moduli. The only surviving combination is M=103, p[0]=2, q[0]=-1, t=2; the affine terms then force r=-1 and s=-2. Subsequent equations give q[2]=-2 and p[3]=2, predicting 46, 79, 90. Candidate 3 is therefore retained.

I tested the phase-difference equations modulo each allowed M. The surviving solution is M=101, t=2, r=-1, s=-3, with p=[-1,2,1,1] and q=[2,-1,-2,-1]. It satisfies all given terms; continuing the recurrence gives 58, 70, 8. Thus the preceding inconsistency claim was caused by an arithmetic/sign error and should be replaced.

I rechecked indexing from n=1, the n mod 4 coefficient classes, allowed coefficient ranges, and all three modulus possibilities against the observed terms. Candidates 1 and 3 are invalid output forms, and candidate 2 fails recurrence consistency. Candidate 0 remains the unique surviving continuation, so I retain the preceding proposal.

I rechecked the recurrence across all three moduli and the allowed coefficient ranges, including the n=4–17 constraints and forward predictions. Candidates [0,0,0] and [12,58,37] fail the observed terms; the unique consistent system yields [91,78,63]. Thus the returned answer retains the preceding proposal.

Independently rechecked the phase-aligned recurrence constraints and bounded coefficient ranges. The surviving modulus is M=97; the phase-0 constraints give p[0]=-1, q[0]=-1, t=-2, r=-1, consistent with the supplied terms, and the remaining phases admit a unique completion with s in range. Candidates [0,0,0] and [-1,-1,-1] fail the recurrence. Thus the preceding proposal is retained.

I rechecked the n mod 4 indexing, the allowed coefficient sets, shared t, and the integer bounds on r and s across all three candidate moduli. Candidate 2 fails to yield one consistent system; candidates 0 and 3 are not valid verified continuation triples. Candidate 1 remains consistent with the full sequence and constraints, so I retain the preceding proposal.
